mappings.rationaleAI: Gödel's incompleteness theorems (1931) are central to 20th-century logic (A.4), contributing to metalogical studies on formal systems' limitations (A.4.c) and developments in predicate and propositional logic (A.4.b). His results on computability and the limits of formal provability directly address questions of logic's limitations and Gödel's incompleteness theorems (B.1.b).

Comprehensive survey of logic from Aristotelian syllogistics through Frege to modern proof systems, covering ancient logic (A.1), medieval developments (A.2), the rise of mathematical logic (A.3), and 20th-century formal systems (A.4). Also addresses the nature and varieties of logic (B.1) and issues in meaning, truth, and alternative logics (B.2).
Aristotle founded formal logic through the Organon, developing theories of syllogism, demonstration, and modal logic — the logic of predicates (A.1.b).

Reason as the cognitive capacity for deductive, inductive, and abductive reasoning connects to conceptions of logic's field and scope (B.1.a) and problems in meaning and truth related to logical semantics (B.2.a).
Philosophy as parent discipline of logic addresses the nature and varieties of logic and differing conceptions of its field and scope (B.1.a).

Surveys the development of logic within broader philosophical traditions, covering Indian logic and epistemology (A.5.a), ancient Greek contributions (A.1), and Islamic Kalam and medieval developments (A.2).
The Islamic Golden Age contributed to medieval logic through Arabic scholars' development and transmission of Aristotelian logic (A.2.a).
Ontology investigates problems of individuation, existence, and the status of abstract objects — directly addressing problems of ontology in the philosophy of logic (B.2.b).
Western philosophy traces the development of logic from ancient Greek rationalism through medieval Scholasticism to early modern mathematical logic (A.1, A.2, A.3).
Eastern philosophy covers Indian logic originating in sutra commentaries and Chinese logic rooted in Confucian, Taoist, and Mohist controversies (A.5.a, A.5.b).

Turing formalized computability and decidability via the Turing machine, contributing to metalogical studies on properties of axiomatized systems (A.4.c) and questions of logic and computability (B.1.b).
Avicenna's Peripatetic philosophy and Book of Healing contributed to medieval logic through Arabic development of Aristotelian logical frameworks (A.2.a).

Mathematical proof as deductive argument from axioms connects to 19th-century development of formal logic and the study of formal systems (A.3.c) and metalogical studies of axiomatized systems (A.4.c).

Set theory under ZFC axioms connects to Cantor's and Frege's work on the relation between logic and foundations of mathematics (A.3.c) and the conflict between Logicism, Intuitionism, and Formalism (A.4.a).
The history of mathematics covers the 19th-century development of set theory and formal logic, including Frege and Cantor's study of the relation between logic and mathematics foundations (A.3.c).
Descartes' method of radical doubt and foundationalism influenced the development of logic during the Renaissance and the rise of mathematical approaches to reasoning (A.3.a).
